planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in wet, mucky, organic-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; skunk cabbage is very slow from seed (3-5 years to first flower) but establishes a near-permanent presence — the contractile roots pull the rhizome deeper into the soil each year, making mature plants essentially impossible to transplant or remove. Site carefully.

directions: Plant in saturated to wet, mucky, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade along stream margins, seepage slopes, and the perpetually wet fringes of woodland ponds; skunk cabbage is one of the most remarkable native plants of eastern North American woodlands — it generates its own heat through a process of thermogenesis, burning stored starch to raise the temperature inside its hooded spathe by 15–35°F above ambient, allowing it to bloom in late winter while snow still covers the ground. This heat volatilizes the fetid, sulfurous compounds that give the plant its name, attracting early-emerging carrion flies as pollinators. The massive, tropical-looking leaves emerge after flowering and can exceed 2 feet across, creating a bold, lush tropical effect in wet, shaded woodland settings. The plant is essentially permanent once established, with contractile roots that prevent removal. A dramatic native for wet shade gardens where its tropical-looking leaves contrast beautifully with ferns and woodland sedges.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in very moist to wet, humus-rich, strongly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fetterbush grows naturally in moist to wet, acidic habitats including pocosins, bay swamps, bog edges, and moist pine flatwoods of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in very moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich, peaty soil in partial shade; fetterbush is a native ericaceous shrub of the southeastern Coastal Plain's wet, acidic habitats — pocosins, bay swamps, wet pine flatwoods, and bog margins from New Jersey to Florida and west to Louisiana. The white, urn-shaped flowers in spring resemble those of its close relative Leucothoe and are equally attractive to early native bees. The foliage turns a rich orange-red in fall, providing excellent autumn color in wet woodland gardens. The shrub spreads slowly by stolons to form colonies, making it useful for stabilizing the margins of wet, shaded areas. As a native member of the heath family (Ericaceae) alongside blueberries and azaleas, it thrives under the same acidic, peaty conditions — making it a natural companion for these plants in moist, shaded settings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; black-edged sedge grows in dry to moderately moist, sandy or rocky, acidic soils in woodland settings across the southeastern US. One of the better sedges for dry-shade conditions.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, sandy or rocky, acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black-edged sedge is a low-growing, clump-forming native sedge of the southeastern piedmont and Coastal Plain, growing in the dry shade of upland oak and pine woodlands where few other grasses or sedges survive. The common and scientific names (nigromarginata = black-margined) refer to the dark margins of the scales in the fruiting spikes, a useful identification feature. This is one of the most genuinely drought-tolerant and shade-tolerant sedges available for difficult dry-shade conditions — a garden situation where moisture-loving sedges like carex appalachica, laxiflora, and platyphylla would struggle. The plant stays very low and compact, spreading slowly into small colonies without becoming aggressive. An excellent ground layer plant for dry, shaded, wooded areas in zone 9a.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall; fringed polygala requires moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained forest soil in deep shade and is notoriously difficult to establish from seed or transplant — it grows in association with specific mycorrhizal fungi. Best acquired as established container plants.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained, undisturbed forest soil in deep shade; fringed polygala is a tiny, jewel-like native groundcover of the cool, moist northern hardwood and mixed conifer forests of eastern North America. The small, rose-pink to magenta flowers have a unique, highly ornamental structure — the two wing-like lateral sepals and the fringed lower petal create a shape resembling a miniature orchid or a tiny winged fairy. The flowers are pollinated by large bumblebees that must be heavy enough to depress the flower's structure to reach the nectar. Two types of flowers are produced: the showy open flowers above ground and hidden cleistogamous flowers underground that self-pollinate without opening. A challenging but rewarding woodland plant for undisturbed, acidic, forest-floor conditions; does not tolerate root disturbance or soil compaction.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions very carefully in fall in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, undisturbed forest soil in deep shade; Appalachian twayblade depends on specific mycorrhizal fungi for establishment and is essentially impossible to grow without these fungal partners present in the native soil. Only plant in existing undisturbed woodland with intact organic duff layer.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, undisturbed woodland soil in deep shade with an intact organic duff layer; Appalachian twayblade is a diminutive native terrestrial orchid of cool, shaded, moist forest floors in the Appalachians and adjacent regions. The plant is distinguished by a single pair of opposite, rounded leaves (hence 'twayblade' — two-blade) at the base of a slender stem bearing tiny greenish flowers. Like all native terrestrial orchids, it depends entirely on specific soil fungi (mycorrhizae) for survival and cannot be successfully grown in sterilized or amended potting soil. The plant's best use in the landscape is simply protecting existing populations in undisturbed woodland rather than attempting cultivation. Listed in some states as a species of conservation concern due to woodland habitat loss. Grow only from ethically sourced, nursery-propagated stock.
